subsystem: TensorFlow image-resize kernel: the templated helper ComputeInterpolationWeights that precomputes per-output-pixel bilinear interpolation coefficients (lower/upper source indices, lerp/ilerp fractional weights) for a (quantized) resize-bilinear operation. It fills an InterpolationCache<T_SCALE> that the resize inner loop later consumes to gather and blend source pixels.
provenance: Identified by structure and naming, not by an SPDX/copyright tag (none present). The int64 typedef, the InterpolationCache<T_SCALE> template struct with parallel lower/upper/lerp/ilerp vectors, the Scaler functor, the fixed-point ilerp built as (frac)*(1<<resolution), and the exact function name ComputeInterpolationWeights are the signature of TensorFlow's resize_bilinear_op / quantized_resize_bilinear_op (tensorflow/core/kernels/image/). The in-band tag 'primevul_validation_0025_vuln' and the file name suffix cwe369 mark this as a PrimeVul divide-by-zero sample.
trust_boundary: The scalar arguments out_size, in_size, scale and resolution originate from the resize op's Compute() method, which derives them from user-supplied tensors: the output height/width come from the attacker-controllable 'size' tensor, in_size from the input tensor shape, and scale from CalculateResizeScale(in_size, out_size, align_corners) which divides by out_size. A caller invoking tf.raw_ops.ResizeBilinear / QuantizedResizeBilinear with size=[0,...] therefore drives out_size==0 into the scale division and into this function's allocation/index arithmetic. Everything reachable from those four scalars is hostile input; the InterpolationCache pointer itself is trusted (allocated by the caller).

what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares an inline function template helper (T_SCALE and the Scaler type are template parameters bound at the call site). out_size is the number of output pixels along the resized axis; in_size is the number of source pixels; scale is the source-to-destination ratio precomputed by the caller (conceptually in_size/out_size); resolution is the number of fractional bits used to build the fixed-point weight; interpolation is an output cache of four parallel vectors that this function fills. All four scalars flow from the op's Compute() and ultimately from user tensors, so their values are attacker-influenced.

Default-constructs a stateless Scaler functor. In TensorFlow this is either HalfPixelScaler (in = (i + 0.5)*scale - 0.5) or LegacyScaler (in = i*scale); it maps an output index i to a floating-point source coordinate using scale. It performs no bounds checking of its own — it simply multiplies by scale — so any pathological scale (inf/nan from a bad division) propagates unchanged through it.

Sizes the four coefficient vectors to out_size + 1 entries each — one per output pixel plus a trailing sentinel slot at index out_size. resize() takes a size_t; the extra +1 is where an int64 out_size interacts badly with unsigned conversion. No validation of out_size is done here (no check that it is positive or bounded), so the allocation size is entirely a function of the untrusted out_size argument.

Writes the sentinel entry at index out_size (the last valid slot given the resize(out_size + 1) above). This is the guard entry the downstream resize loop reads when it needs a one-past-the-end boundary. It is index-correct only as long as the resize on lines 6-9 actually produced out_size + 1 elements, i.e. only if the out_size + 1 conversion did not wrap or throw.

Iterates output indices from out_size-1 down to 0. For each, it maps the output index to a fractional source coordinate 'in' via the Scaler using scale, then takes its floor 'in_f'. Note that if out_size <= 0 the loop body never runs (i starts negative), so the pathological small-out_size cases are handled entirely by the allocation and sentinel writes above, not here. 'in' inherits any inf/nan produced upstream when scale is degenerate.

Computes the two integer source indices that bracket the fractional coordinate: lower is floor(in) clamped to be >= 0, upper is ceil(in) clamped to be <= in_size-1. This clamp is what normally keeps subsequent pixel gathers in bounds. It relies on in being a finite number: casting a non-finite float (inf/nan) to int64 is undefined behavior in C++, so the clamp does NOT rescue a degenerate 'in' — std::max/std::min operate on an already-undefined cast result.

Stores the fractional weight lerp = in - floor(in) in [0,1), and its fixed-point form ilerp = frac * (1 << resolution) cast to the integer T_SCALE. The shift (1 << resolution) builds the scale factor (e.g. 1<<8 = 256 for an 8-bit fixed-point weight). resolution is an int argument; the shift is performed on int (value 1), so the result and its validity depend entirely on resolution staying in a small positive range.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const int64 out_size, const int64 in_size, const float scale,
    const int resolution, InterpolationCache<T_SCALE>* interpolation) {
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: LATENT DIVIDE-BY-ZERO (the labeled cwe369 defect). scale is not computed here but by the caller as CalculateResizeScale(in_size, out_size, align_corners), which returns in_size / static_cast<float>(out_size) when not aligning corners. The output dimension out_size comes from the user-supplied 'size' tensor and is never validated to be > 0 before that division. With size=[0,...] the caller divides by zero producing scale=+inf (or nan for 0/0), which this function then feeds into scaler(i, scale) at line 14. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: reject out_height/out_width <= 0 (OP_REQUIRES) BEFORE computing any resize scale. TensorFlow's fix for this op family added exactly such positivity guards in Compute(); this helper trusts a pre-divided scale and cannot defend itself.

Non-finite float to integer conversion (downstream of the divide-by-zero). When scale is inf/nan, 'in' and std::ceil(in)/std::floor(in) are inf/nan, and static_cast<int64>(inf) / static_cast<int64>(nan) is undefined behavior — on common targets it yields INT64_MIN or an arbitrary value. The std::max/std::min clamps do not help because they run on the already-undefined result, so interpolation->upper[i] can become a wild negative or huge index. The downstream resize gather uses these indices to address the source image, turning a bad output size into an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). The invariant: 'in' must be finite before any cast; equivalently, scale must be finite, which again requires out_size > 0.

Signed shift / integer-overflow hazard. resolution is a plain int passed in from the caller. (1 << resolution) shifts the int literal 1; if resolution >= 31 the shift overflows a 32-bit signed int (undefined behavior, CWE-190/CWE-1335), and if resolution is negative the shift is likewise undefined. Even when defined, an oversized resolution makes the fixed-point weight overflow T_SCALE on the cast. The invariant: resolution must be a small, non-negative, statically-bounded constant (typically the fixed-point bit-width, e.g. 8); the function does not enforce this and simply trusts the argument.

Unvalidated size / integer-overflow in the allocation length. out_size is an untrusted int64. A negative out_size (e.g. from a malformed or overflowed size computation) makes out_size + 1 negative; converted to the size_t parameter of resize() it becomes an enormous value, throwing std::bad_alloc / std::length_error and crashing the process (DoS), or, at the exact boundary out_size == INT64_MAX, out_size + 1 signed-overflows (UB) before the conversion. The invariant: validate 0 < out_size <= a sane cap before using it as an allocation count, and compute the +1 in an unsigned/overflow-checked type.

summary: A TensorFlow resize-bilinear coefficient helper that trusts four caller-supplied scalars without validation. The central, labeled defect is a latent divide-by-zero: scale is produced upstream by dividing by the user-controlled output size (out_size) with no out_size>0 guard, yielding inf/nan that then flows into non-finite float->int64 casts and bad interpolation indices (downstream OOB read). Secondary hazards are an unchecked (1<<resolution) shift (CWE-190) and an unvalidated out_size+1 allocation length (CWE-190/DoS).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point taking untrusted out_size/in_size/scale/resolution; carries the divide-by-zero and validation contracts.
Produced by an upstream division by out_size (CalculateResizeScale); divide-by-zero / non-finite value source — the primary cwe369 defect.
Untrusted output dimension; unvalidated, drives both the divisor upstream and the out_size+1 allocation length.
Used only as an upper clamp (in_size - 1); a bad in_size narrows/degrades the clamp but is not itself the divisor or an allocation size in this function.
Used as a shift count in (1 << resolution); unbounded/negative values are undefined behavior.
Output cache allocated and owned by the trusted caller; not attacker-controllable and always non-null in this call path.
Stateless coordinate functor; performs no bounds logic and adds no independent contract beyond propagating scale.
Allocation length is untrusted out_size+1 with no positivity/overflow check; negative/extreme values crash or wrap.
Index-correct given resize(out_size+1); only unsafe if the resize itself wrapped, which is captured by the allocation finding.
Loop bound is safe: for out_size<=0 the body never runs; the degenerate cases live in the scale division and allocation, not the loop.
Pure float math; the hazard is the non-finite value it propagates, attributed to the scale/divide-by-zero finding.
Casting non-finite float (from inf/nan scale) to int64 is UB and defeats the max/min clamp, producing wild source indices (downstream OOB read).
Shift count is an untrusted int; >=31 or negative is undefined behavior and can overflow T_SCALE.

findings.title: Divide-by-zero from unvalidated output size feeding the resize scale
findings.anchor: const float scale parameter, consumed at scaler(i, scale)
findings.code:
const int64 out_size, const int64 in_size, const float scale,
    const float in = scaler(i, scale);
findings.bug_class: Division by zero producing non-finite scale (then UB float->int cast)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the caller's CalculateResizeScale / resize op Compute()
findings.invariant: The output dimension out_size (and thus the divisor when computing scale = in_size / out_size) must be validated to be strictly > 0 BEFORE the division; equivalently scale must be guaranteed finite before it reaches this function.
findings.failure_mode: A user invokes the resize op with an output size of 0 (size=[0,...]). The caller computes scale = in_size / static_cast<float>(0) = +inf (or 0/0 = nan). This function feeds scale into scaler(i, scale) at line 14, yielding a non-finite 'in'. The subsequent std::floor/std::ceil and static_cast<int64> at lines 16-19 then execute undefined float->int conversions, corrupting the lower/upper source indices; downstream pixel gathers read out of bounds. Impact: process crash / OOB read (denial of service, potential info leak) reachable from a single crafted op call.
findings.what_to_confirm: In the calling kernel (resize_bilinear_op.cc / quantized_resize_bilinear_op.cc), confirm whether Compute() has an OP_REQUIRES that out_height>0 and out_width>0 before CalculateResizeScale, and that CalculateResizeScale guards out_size==0.

findings.title: Non-finite float cast to int64 defeats the index clamp
findings.bug_class: Undefined non-finite float-to-integer conversion; broken bounds clamp
findings.invariant: 'in' must be a finite value before any static_cast<int64>; the max/min clamp only bounds finite inputs, so scale must be finite for the clamp to keep indices within [0, in_size-1].
findings.failure_mode: When the divide-by-zero above makes scale inf/nan, 'in', std::floor(in) and std::ceil(in) are inf/nan and static_cast<int64>(inf/nan) is undefined behavior (commonly INT64_MIN on x86). std::max/std::min then clamp a garbage value, so interpolation->upper[i] can end up negative or gigantic. The resize inner loop later uses these as source-pixel offsets, producing an out-of-bounds read of the input image.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no upstream guarantee that scale is finite, and that the gather step using interpolation->lower/upper does not re-clamp indices before dereferencing the source buffer.

findings.title: Unbounded shift count in fixed-point weight construction
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow / undefined shift (shift count out of range)
findings.invariant: resolution must be a small, non-negative constant strictly less than the bit-width of int (and small enough that (1<<resolution) fits T_SCALE); it must never be attacker-derived.
findings.failure_mode: resolution is a plain int argument. If a caller passes resolution >= 31, (1 << resolution) shifts a 32-bit signed 1 past its width — undefined behavior; a negative resolution is likewise UB. Even in the defined range, an oversized resolution overflows the fixed-point weight when cast to T_SCALE, corrupting every interpolation coefficient. Impact ranges from wrong results to sanitizer-detected UB / miscompiled bounds later.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the call site always passes a compile-time constant resolution (e.g. 8) and that it is never derived from tensor data or attributes.

findings.title: Unvalidated output size used as allocation length (out_size + 1)
findings.invariant: out_size must be validated as 0 < out_size <= a sane maximum before being used as a vector length, and the +1 must be computed without signed overflow.
findings.failure_mode: out_size is an untrusted int64 with no range check. A negative out_size makes out_size+1 negative, which converts to a huge size_t in resize() and throws std::length_error/std::bad_alloc (uncaught -> process abort). At the exact value INT64_MAX, out_size+1 signed-overflows (UB) before conversion. Either way an attacker who influences the output dimension can force an allocation-time crash.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the op validates out_height/out_width as positive and bounded before calling this helper, so out_size can never be negative or near INT64_MAX here.

audit_checklist: In the resize op Compute(), confirm OP_REQUIRES guards that out_height>0 and out_width>0 (and in_height/in_width>0) before CalculateResizeScale, so no division by zero can occur.
Confirm CalculateResizeScale (or wherever scale is computed) cannot return inf/nan for any user-supplied out_size, including out_size==0 and out_size==1 with align_corners.
Verify that the source-pixel gather consuming interpolation->lower/upper re-validates indices against the input extent, so a corrupted upper[i] cannot cause an OOB read.
Confirm resolution is always a fixed, small, non-negative compile-time constant and never derived from tensor data or op attributes.
Verify out_size is range-checked (0 < out_size <= cap) before resize(out_size + 1), and that the +1 cannot overflow.
Add a finiteness assertion on 'in'/scale (or reject non-finite scale) before the static_cast<int64> conversions on lines 16-19.
